fallback.description: This detected label is not yet defined in the fallacy reference library. Review the highlighted passage directly instead of relying on a category badge.

aliases.Quoting Out of Context: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context

relatedness_schema.same_family: Broader logical/rhetorical buckets used for close similarity.
relatedness_schema.related: Curated fallacies that are logically close.
relatedness_schema.often_with: Curated fallacies that frequently appear in tandem.
relatedness_schema.confused_with: Fallacies that are commonly mistaken for one another.

Affirming the Consequent.description: Treats a result as proof of a particular cause, even though the same result could follow from other causes too.
examples: If the alarm is set, the office will be locked. The office is locked, so the alarm must have been set.
Affirming the Consequent.explanation: Treats a result as proof of a particular cause, even though the same result could follow from other causes too. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Affirming the Consequent.name: Affirming the Consequent

Denying the Antecedent.description: Assumes that if a condition is absent, the outcome must also be absent, even though the outcome could still arise in other ways.
examples: If the file is in the cloud, I can open it from home. The file is not in the cloud, so I cannot open it from home.
Denying the Antecedent.explanation: Assumes that if a condition is absent, the outcome must also be absent, even though the outcome could still arise in other ways. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Denying the Antecedent.name: Denying the Antecedent

Undistributed Middle.description: Uses a shared middle term in a syllogism without properly connecting the categories, so the conclusion does not follow.
examples: All poets use language. My neighbor uses language. Therefore my neighbor is a poet.
Undistributed Middle.explanation: Uses a shared middle term in a syllogism without properly connecting the categories, so the conclusion does not follow. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Undistributed Middle.name: Undistributed Middle

Illicit Major.description: Draws a syllogistic conclusion that distributes the major term more broadly than the premises allow.
examples: All cats are mammals. No dogs are cats. Therefore no dogs are mammals.
Illicit Major.explanation: Draws a syllogistic conclusion that distributes the major term more broadly than the premises allow. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Illicit Major.name: Illicit Major

Illicit Minor.description: Draws a syllogistic conclusion that distributes the minor term more broadly than the premises allow.
examples: All cats are mammals. Some pets are mammals. Therefore all pets are cats.
Illicit Minor.explanation: Draws a syllogistic conclusion that distributes the minor term more broadly than the premises allow. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Illicit Minor.name: Illicit Minor

Four-Term Fallacy.description: Uses a syllogism with four terms instead of three, often because a key term shifts meaning.
examples: Feathers are light. What is light cannot be dark. Therefore feathers cannot be dark.
Four-Term Fallacy.explanation: Uses a syllogism with four terms instead of three, often because a key term shifts meaning. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Four-Term Fallacy.name: Four-Term Fallacy

Propositional Fallacies.description: A broader label for invalid moves in propositional logic where the conclusion does not validly follow from the premises.
examples: Either the plan works or the team is lazy. The plan did not work, so the team must be lazy.
Propositional Fallacies.explanation: A broader label for invalid moves in propositional logic where the conclusion does not validly follow from the premises. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Propositional Fallacies.name: Propositional Fallacies

Quantification Fallacies.description: Misuses words like all, some, or none so the conclusion goes beyond what the premises support.
examples: Everyone in the room solved one problem, so one person in the room solved every problem.
Quantification Fallacies.explanation: Misuses words like all, some, or none so the conclusion goes beyond what the premises support. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Quantification Fallacies.name: Quantification Fallacies

Syllogistic Fallacies.description: Uses a broken syllogism or category relationship so the conclusion does not logically follow.
examples: Some artists are teachers. Some teachers are critics. Therefore some artists are critics.
Syllogistic Fallacies.explanation: Uses a broken syllogism or category relationship so the conclusion does not logically follow. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Syllogistic Fallacies.name: Syllogistic Fallacies

Bad Reason Fallacy.description: Offers reasons that do not actually justify the conclusion being claimed.
examples: This policy must be good because it sounds bold and confident.
Bad Reason Fallacy.explanation: Offers reasons that do not actually justify the conclusion being claimed. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Bad Reason Fallacy.name: Bad Reason Fallacy

Sunk Cost Fallacy.description: Keeps defending or continuing something mainly because time, money, or effort has already been spent on it.
examples: We have already spent six months on this failing app, so we have to keep funding it.
Sunk Cost Fallacy.explanation: Keeps defending or continuing something mainly because time, money, or effort has already been spent on it. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Sunk Cost Fallacy.name: Sunk Cost Fallacy

Ad Hominem.description: Attacks the person making the argument instead of addressing the argument itself.
examples: Do not listen to her argument about transit policy; she rents an apartment and does not even own a car.
Ad Hominem.explanation: Attacks the person making the argument instead of addressing the argument itself. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Ad Hominem.name: Ad Hominem

Ambiguity.description: Relies on vague or slippery wording that can be taken in more than one way.
examples: The sign says the museum is free, so everything inside should cost nothing.
Ambiguity.explanation: Relies on vague or slippery wording that can be taken in more than one way. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
related: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context
Ambiguity.name: Ambiguity

Anecdotal.description: Uses a personal story or isolated example as if it were enough to prove a broad claim.
examples: My uncle smoked every day and lived to ninety, so smoking risks are exaggerated.
Anecdotal.explanation: Uses a personal story or isolated example as if it were enough to prove a broad claim. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Anecdotal.name: Anecdotal

Appeal to Authority.description: Treats a claim as true mainly because an authority figure or expert said it, without enough supporting evidence.
examples: A famous actor says this supplement works, so it must be medically effective.
Appeal to Authority.explanation: Treats a claim as true mainly because an authority figure or expert said it, without enough supporting evidence. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Authority.name: Appeal to Authority

Appeal to Emotion.description: Pushes the audience toward a conclusion by provoking emotion rather than giving solid reasons.
examples: If you really cared about children, you would support this bill without asking for data.
Appeal to Emotion.explanation: Pushes the audience toward a conclusion by provoking emotion rather than giving solid reasons. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Emotion.name: Appeal to Emotion

Appeal to Fear.description: Leans on fear or alarm to drive acceptance of a claim instead of supporting it with sound reasoning.
examples: If we do not approve this measure today, chaos will spread through the entire city by next week.
Appeal to Fear.explanation: Leans on fear or alarm to drive acceptance of a claim instead of supporting it with sound reasoning. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Fear.name: Appeal to Fear

Appeal to Pity.description: Treats sympathy or compassion as if it were evidence that a claim is true or justified.
examples: Please give me a passing grade; I have had a very difficult month.
Appeal to Pity.explanation: Treats sympathy or compassion as if it were evidence that a claim is true or justified. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Pity.name: Appeal to Pity

Appeal to Force.description: Uses threats, coercion, or the prospect of punishment in place of actual reasons.
examples: Agree with the proposal, or you can forget about your promotion.
Appeal to Force.explanation: Uses threats, coercion, or the prospect of punishment in place of actual reasons. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Force.name: Appeal to Force

Appeal to Ignorance.description: Claims something is true or false simply because it has not been proven otherwise.
examples: No one has proven that the software is unsafe, so it must be safe.
Appeal to Ignorance.explanation: Claims something is true or false simply because it has not been proven otherwise. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Ignorance.name: Appeal to Ignorance

Appeal to Nature.description: Assumes something is good, right, or safer simply because it is called natural.
examples: This remedy is natural, so it has to be healthier than the prescription drug.
Appeal to Nature.explanation: Assumes something is good, right, or safer simply because it is called natural. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Nature.name: Appeal to Nature

Appeal to Probability.description: Assumes that because something could happen or seems likely, it therefore will happen.
examples: With so many talented people on the team, success is basically guaranteed.
Appeal to Probability.explanation: Assumes that because something could happen or seems likely, it therefore will happen. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Probability.name: Appeal to Probability

Appeal to Ridicule.description: Mocks a position to make it seem false instead of actually refuting it.
examples: Only an idiot would think that public libraries need more funding.
Appeal to Ridicule.explanation: Mocks a position to make it seem false instead of actually refuting it. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Ridicule.name: Appeal to Ridicule

Appeal to Tradition.description: Claims something is correct or preferable mainly because it has long been done that way.
examples: We should keep doing it this way because this is how the department has always done it.
Appeal to Tradition.explanation: Claims something is correct or preferable mainly because it has long been done that way. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Tradition.name: Appeal to Tradition

Appeal to Consequences.description: Treats desirable or undesirable consequences as proof that a claim itself is true or false.
examples: Climate change cannot be real, because if it were real the future would be too frightening.
Appeal to Consequences.explanation: Treats desirable or undesirable consequences as proof that a claim itself is true or false. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Appeal to Consequences.name: Appeal to Consequences

Argument from Repetition.description: Repeats a claim so often that the repetition itself is used to make it feel true.
examples: The ad says the product is revolutionary ten times in a row, so people start taking it as established fact.
Argument from Repetition.explanation: Repeats a claim so often that the repetition itself is used to make it feel true. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Argument from Repetition.name: Argument from Repetition

Argumentum ad Populum.description: Treats popularity or public approval as evidence that a claim is true.
examples: Millions of people share this claim online, so it must be true.
Argumentum ad Populum.explanation: Treats popularity or public approval as evidence that a claim is true. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Argumentum ad Populum.name: Argumentum ad Populum

Bandwagon.description: Urges acceptance of a claim because many people supposedly already accept it.
examples: Everyone in the industry is adopting this tool, so we would be foolish not to accept it as the best option.
Bandwagon.explanation: Urges acceptance of a claim because many people supposedly already accept it. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Bandwagon.name: Bandwagon

Begging the Question.description: Builds the conclusion into the premise, so the argument assumes what it is trying to prove.
examples: This newspaper is trustworthy because it reports the news reliably.
Begging the Question.explanation: Builds the conclusion into the premise, so the argument assumes what it is trying to prove. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Begging the Question.name: Begging the Question

Burden of Proof.description: Shifts responsibility for proving a claim onto other people instead of supporting it directly.
examples: Prove that my conspiracy theory is false; until then, I am justified in believing it.
Burden of Proof.explanation: Shifts responsibility for proving a claim onto other people instead of supporting it directly. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Burden of Proof.name: Burden of Proof

Cherry Picking.description: Selects only the evidence that supports a conclusion while ignoring relevant evidence that points the other way.
examples: The candidate highlights the three months of job growth but ignores the longer downward trend.
Cherry Picking.explanation: Selects only the evidence that supports a conclusion while ignoring relevant evidence that points the other way. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
related: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context
confused_with: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context
Cherry Picking.name: Cherry Picking

Circular Reasoning.description: Restates the conclusion in different words instead of supplying independent support for it.
examples: The rule is fair because the policy says it is fair, and the policy is correct because it reflects our fair rules.
Circular Reasoning.explanation: Restates the conclusion in different words instead of supplying independent support for it. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Circular Reasoning.name: Circular Reasoning

Composition Fallacy.description: Assumes that what is true of the parts must also be true of the whole.
examples: Each player on the team is a star, so the team must be unbeatable.
Composition Fallacy.explanation: Assumes that what is true of the parts must also be true of the whole. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Composition Fallacy.name: Composition Fallacy

Continuum Fallacy.description: Claims a distinction is meaningless just because the boundary between cases is gradual or fuzzy.
examples: Because there is no exact second when dusk becomes night, day and night are not really different things.
Continuum Fallacy.explanation: Claims a distinction is meaningless just because the boundary between cases is gradual or fuzzy. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Continuum Fallacy.name: Continuum Fallacy

Division Fallacy.description: Assumes that what is true of the whole must also be true of each part.
examples: The company is wealthy, so every employee there must be wealthy too.
Division Fallacy.explanation: Assumes that what is true of the whole must also be true of each part. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Division Fallacy.name: Division Fallacy

Equivocation.description: Switches the meaning of a key word or phrase in the middle of an argument.
examples: Feathers are light. What is light cannot be dark. Therefore feathers cannot be dark.
Equivocation.explanation: Switches the meaning of a key word or phrase in the middle of an argument. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Equivocation.name: Equivocation

Etymological Fallacy.description: Assumes a word’s current meaning or truth is determined by its historical origin.
examples: The word nice used to mean foolish, so calling someone nice must still imply foolishness.
Etymological Fallacy.explanation: Assumes a word’s current meaning or truth is determined by its historical origin. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Etymological Fallacy.name: Etymological Fallacy

Fallacy Fallacy.description: Assumes a claim must be false merely because the argument for it was weak or fallacious.
examples: That activist used a bad argument once, so the cause itself must be wrong.
Fallacy Fallacy.explanation: Assumes a claim must be false merely because the argument for it was weak or fallacious. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Fallacy Fallacy.name: Fallacy Fallacy

Fallacy of Composition and Division.description: Improperly transfers a property from parts to the whole, or from the whole to the parts.
examples: Because the neighborhood is expensive, every item sold in every shop there must be expensive.
Fallacy of Composition and Division.explanation: Improperly transfers a property from parts to the whole, or from the whole to the parts. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Fallacy of Composition and Division.name: Fallacy of Composition and Division

Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context.description: Pulls words from their surrounding context so they appear to mean something different.
examples: A sentence from a long interview is isolated so the speaker appears to support the opposite of what the full passage says.
Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context.explanation: Pulls words from their surrounding context so they appear to mean something different. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context.name: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context

False Analogy.description: Treats two things as more alike than they really are, using a weak comparison to support the conclusion.
examples: Running a country is just like running a household, so national budgets should work exactly the same way.
False Analogy.explanation: Treats two things as more alike than they really are, using a weak comparison to support the conclusion. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
False Analogy.name: False Analogy

False Attribution.description: Assigns a statement, motive, source, or cause to the wrong person or factor.
examples: A quote is assigned to Einstein online, so readers assume it carries scientific authority.
False Attribution.explanation: Assigns a statement, motive, source, or cause to the wrong person or factor. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
related: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context
confused_with: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context
False Attribution.name: False Attribution

False Cause.description: Treats two things as causally connected without enough evidence that one really caused the other.
examples: I wore my lucky jacket and then we won, so the jacket caused the victory.
False Cause.explanation: Treats two things as causally connected without enough evidence that one really caused the other. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
False Cause.name: False Cause

False Cause & False Attribution.description: Treats two things as causally connected, or assigns credit or blame, without enough evidence.
examples: The speaker credits one policy for economic recovery while ignoring the larger set of forces that changed at the same time.
False Cause & False Attribution.explanation: Treats two things as causally connected, or assigns credit or blame, without enough evidence. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
False Cause & False Attribution.name: False Cause & False Attribution

False Dilemma.description: Frames a situation as if there were only two options when more possibilities exist.
examples: Either you support this proposal exactly as written, or you do not care about the problem at all.
False Dilemma.explanation: Frames a situation as if there were only two options when more possibilities exist. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
False Dilemma.name: False Dilemma

False Equivalence.description: Treats two things as morally, logically, or practically equivalent when the differences matter.
examples: Misplacing one receipt is treated as morally identical to large-scale financial fraud.
False Equivalence.explanation: Treats two things as morally, logically, or practically equivalent when the differences matter. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
False Equivalence.name: False Equivalence

Faulty Generalization.description: Draws a broad rule or conclusion from too little, too narrow, or unrepresentative evidence.
examples: Two rude tourists were from one country, so people from that country must be rude.
Faulty Generalization.explanation: Draws a broad rule or conclusion from too little, too narrow, or unrepresentative evidence. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Faulty Generalization.name: Faulty Generalization

Furtive Fallacy.description: Assumes hidden intent, secret coordination, or deliberate design without sufficient evidence.
examples: The policy changed quietly, which proves there was a secret plot behind it.
Furtive Fallacy.explanation: Assumes hidden intent, secret coordination, or deliberate design without sufficient evidence. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Furtive Fallacy.name: Furtive Fallacy

Gambler's Fallacy.description: Assumes past random outcomes make a different future outcome more likely, even when events are independent.
examples: The coin has landed on heads five times, so tails is now due.
Gambler's Fallacy.explanation: Assumes past random outcomes make a different future outcome more likely, even when events are independent. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Gambler's Fallacy.name: Gambler's Fallacy

Genetic Fallacy.description: Judges a claim by where it came from rather than by the quality of the claim itself.
examples: The idea came from social media, so it cannot possibly be valid.
Genetic Fallacy.explanation: Judges a claim by where it came from rather than by the quality of the claim itself. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Genetic Fallacy.name: Genetic Fallacy

Guilt by Association.description: Dismisses a person or claim by linking it to someone or something disliked rather than addressing the reasoning itself.
examples: He once attended the same event as a fraudster, so his own argument must be dishonest too.
Guilt by Association.explanation: Dismisses a person or claim by linking it to someone or something disliked rather than addressing the reasoning itself. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Guilt by Association.name: Guilt by Association

Hasty Generalization.description: Jumps from a small or unrepresentative sample to a sweeping conclusion.
examples: I met two careless drivers from that city, so drivers from there are reckless.
Hasty Generalization.explanation: Jumps from a small or unrepresentative sample to a sweeping conclusion. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Hasty Generalization.name: Hasty Generalization

Ignoratio Elenchi.description: Presents reasons that may sound relevant but do not actually answer the point at issue.
examples: Asked whether the policy reduces emissions, the speaker answers by saying the country needs jobs.
Ignoratio Elenchi.explanation: Presents reasons that may sound relevant but do not actually answer the point at issue. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Ignoratio Elenchi.name: Ignoratio Elenchi

Incomplete Comparison.description: Makes a comparison without stating what the thing is being compared against or by which standard.
examples: This is the stronger product.
Incomplete Comparison.explanation: Makes a comparison without stating what the thing is being compared against or by which standard. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Incomplete Comparison.name: Incomplete Comparison

Inflation of Conflict.description: Presents a disagreement as a dramatic clash when the actual gap may be smaller or more nuanced.
examples: A modest policy disagreement is described as a battle for the soul of civilization.
Inflation of Conflict.explanation: Presents a disagreement as a dramatic clash when the actual gap may be smaller or more nuanced. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Inflation of Conflict.name: Inflation of Conflict

Kettle Logic.description: Uses mutually inconsistent defenses all at once, hoping one of them will stick.
examples: I did not take your book; it was already broken when I borrowed it; and anyway you never really wanted it back.
Kettle Logic.explanation: Uses mutually inconsistent defenses all at once, hoping one of them will stick. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Kettle Logic.name: Kettle Logic

Loaded Question.description: Asks a question that smuggles in an unproven assumption.
examples: Why are you still ignoring the evidence?
Loaded Question.explanation: Asks a question that smuggles in an unproven assumption. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Loaded Question.name: Loaded Question

Middle Ground.description: Assumes the compromise position must be correct simply because it sits between two extremes.
examples: One expert says the bridge is safe and another says it is unsafe, so the truth must be that it is only half unsafe.
Middle Ground.explanation: Assumes the compromise position must be correct simply because it sits between two extremes. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Middle Ground.name: Middle Ground

Motte and Bailey.description: Retreats to a safer, easier claim when challenged, then returns to the stronger controversial claim once the pressure passes.
examples: When challenged on a sweeping claim about censorship, the speaker retreats to the safer claim that moderation exists online, then returns to the sweeping claim later.
Motte and Bailey.explanation: Retreats to a safer, easier claim when challenged, then returns to the stronger controversial claim once the pressure passes. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Motte and Bailey.name: Motte and Bailey

Moving the Goalposts.description: Changes the standard of proof or success after the original standard has been met.
examples: After the study answers the first objection, the critic says the result only counts if three more unrelated conditions are met.
Moving the Goalposts.explanation: Changes the standard of proof or success after the original standard has been met. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Moving the Goalposts.name: Moving the Goalposts

Nirvana Fallacy.description: Rejects a realistic option because it is not perfect, comparing it unfairly to an idealized alternative.
examples: Because no transportation system can eliminate accidents entirely, there is no point in improving road safety.
Nirvana Fallacy.explanation: Rejects a realistic option because it is not perfect, comparing it unfairly to an idealized alternative. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Nirvana Fallacy.name: Nirvana Fallacy

No True Scotsman.description: Protects a general claim by redefining the group whenever a counterexample appears.
examples: No true fan would ever criticize the team; if you criticize it, you were never a real fan.
No True Scotsman.explanation: Protects a general claim by redefining the group whenever a counterexample appears. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
No True Scotsman.name: No True Scotsman

Personal Incredulity.description: Treats something as false simply because it seems hard to imagine, understand, or believe.
examples: I cannot imagine how evolution could work, so it must be false.
Personal Incredulity.explanation: Treats something as false simply because it seems hard to imagine, understand, or believe. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Personal Incredulity.name: Personal Incredulity

Poisoning the Well.description: Discredits a person in advance so that anything they say is dismissed before it is even considered.
examples: Before she even speaks, we are told not to trust her because she is an outsider.
Poisoning the Well.explanation: Discredits a person in advance so that anything they say is dismissed before it is even considered. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Poisoning the Well.name: Poisoning the Well

Post Hoc.description: Assumes that because one thing happened after another, the first thing must have caused the second.
examples: The mayor took office in January and crime dropped in March, so the mayor alone caused the drop.
Post Hoc.explanation: Assumes that because one thing happened after another, the first thing must have caused the second. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Post Hoc.name: Post Hoc

Proof by Verbosity.description: Uses a flood of words, complexity, or detail to create the impression of proof without actually proving the point.
examples: A speaker buries a weak claim under a long wall of technical-sounding language and treats length as proof.
Proof by Verbosity.explanation: Uses a flood of words, complexity, or detail to create the impression of proof without actually proving the point. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Proof by Verbosity.name: Proof by Verbosity

Proving Too Much.description: Uses reasoning that, if accepted, would justify far more than the speaker likely intends.
examples: If one exception means the rule is useless, then no general rule could ever guide action.
Proving Too Much.explanation: Uses reasoning that, if accepted, would justify far more than the speaker likely intends. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Proving Too Much.name: Proving Too Much

Red Herring.description: Introduces a distraction that pulls attention away from the main issue.
examples: When asked about corruption, the minister starts talking about how beautiful the country is in spring.
Red Herring.explanation: Introduces a distraction that pulls attention away from the main issue. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Red Herring.name: Red Herring

Reification.description: Treats an abstract idea or label as if it were a concrete thing with independent existence.
examples: The market wants stability, so we must obey what the market wants.
Reification.explanation: Treats an abstract idea or label as if it were a concrete thing with independent existence. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Reification.name: Reification

Retrospective Determinism.description: Looks back at past events as if the outcome had been inevitable all along.
examples: After the startup succeeds, commentators say the outcome was inevitable from the first day.
Retrospective Determinism.explanation: Looks back at past events as if the outcome had been inevitable all along. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Retrospective Determinism.name: Retrospective Determinism

Shotgun Argumentation.description: Throws out many weak points at once so they become difficult to answer individually.
examples: The debater fires off twenty weak claims in rapid sequence so opponents cannot answer each one carefully.
Shotgun Argumentation.explanation: Throws out many weak points at once so they become difficult to answer individually. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Shotgun Argumentation.name: Shotgun Argumentation

Slippery Slope.description: Claims a relatively small first step will inevitably trigger a chain of extreme consequences.
examples: If we allow one late assignment, soon deadlines will mean nothing and the whole course will collapse.
Slippery Slope.explanation: Claims a relatively small first step will inevitably trigger a chain of extreme consequences. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Slippery Slope.name: Slippery Slope

Special Pleading.description: Applies standards selectively, carving out an exception when the rule becomes inconvenient.
examples: The rule applies to everyone else, but my case is different and should not be judged by the same standard.
Special Pleading.explanation: Applies standards selectively, carving out an exception when the rule becomes inconvenient. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Special Pleading.name: Special Pleading

Strawman.description: Misrepresents an opposing view so it becomes easier to dismiss.
examples: She says we should regulate the industry, and he replies that she wants to destroy every business in the country.
Strawman.explanation: Misrepresents an opposing view so it becomes easier to dismiss. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
often_with: Fallacy of Quoting Out of Context
Strawman.name: Strawman

Texas Sharpshooter.description: Cherry-picks a pattern or cluster after the fact while ignoring the larger body of evidence.
examples: After scanning the data, the speaker circles one convenient cluster and presents it as the real pattern.
Texas Sharpshooter.explanation: Cherry-picks a pattern or cluster after the fact while ignoring the larger body of evidence. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Texas Sharpshooter.name: Texas Sharpshooter

Tu Quoque.description: Responds to criticism by accusing the critic of similar behavior instead of addressing the criticism.
examples: You say littering is wrong, but I saw you drop a cup last week, so your criticism does not matter.
Tu Quoque.explanation: Responds to criticism by accusing the critic of similar behavior instead of addressing the criticism. In practice, the key question is whether the conclusion is supported by evidence and logic, or whether it depends on this reasoning shortcut instead.
Tu Quoque.name: Tu Quoque
